vulnerability.title: Force frequent session key renegotiation
vulnerability.description: The RekeyLimit parameter specifies how often the session key of the is renegotiated, both in terms of amount of data that may be transmitted and the time elapsed. To decrease the default limits, add or correct the following line in /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf: RekeyLimit
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then var_rekey_limit_size='' var_rekey_limit_time='' mkdir -p /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d touch /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf chmod 0600 /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*RekeyLimit\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config" LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*RekeyLimit\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d"/*.conf if [ -e "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf" ] ; then LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*RekeyLimit\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf" else touch "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf" fi # make sure file has newline at the end sed -i -e '$a\' "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardenin

triage_decision.reason: The remediation only edits an SSH daemon configuration file to set RekeyLimit, a standard STIG hardening parameter. This is a simple text change that does not affect boot or critical services and is unlikely to cause lockout if validated before applying.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; networking affected; ssh_configuration; potential_service_disruption

reasoning_messages.reasoning:
The finding requires a `RekeyLimit` entry in `/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf`. The script’s variables for size and time are empty, so we must supply sensible defaults. The typical default is `1G 1h`. Therefore, plan to edit the file, add or correct the line to `RekeyLimit 1G 1h`, review that the syntax is correct, and then apply the change by restarting or reloading sshd. This satisfies the remediation without risking lock‑out.

I read the target config file. If it’s missing, I create an empty file with mode 0600. To guarantee a single, uncommented “RekeyLimit 1G 1h” line, I first delete any existing RekeyLimit entries (commented or not) with a case‑insensitive sed command, then append the desired line. Each operation is performed separately using `run_cmd`.

First ensure the target directory exists with `mkdir -p /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d`.  
Then create (or touch) the compliance file and set secure permissions: `touch /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf && chmod 0600 …`.  
Next, remove any existing `RekeyLimit` entries to avoid duplication: `sed -i '/^\s*RekeyLimit\s\+/Id' …`.  
Finally, append the required line: `echo "RekeyLimit 1G 1h" >> …`.

review_feedback:
QA output was not valid JSON. Raw: {
  "finding_id": "sshd_rekey_limit",
  "safe": true,
  "verdict_reason": "The plan correctly creates or ensures the hardening config file, removes any existing RekeyLimit entries, and appends a secur...

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan correctly creates/ensures the hardening config file, removes any existing RekeyLimit entries, and appends a secure default. It will address the vulnerability once the SSH daemon reloads the configuration. Minor improvements: explicitly reload/restart sshd, ensure newline handling, and use a precise sed pattern.
concerns: No sshd service reload/restart is included, so the new setting may not take effect until later
Append command may not guarantee a leading newline if file lacks one
Sed pattern not specified could unintentionally remove other lines
suggested_improvements: Add a command to reload sshd (e.g., systemctl reload sshd) after editing
Ensure the file ends with a newline before appending, e.g., using sed -i -e '$a\'
Use a precise sed expression to delete only RekeyLimit lines, e.g., sed -i '/^\s*#\?\s*RekeyLimit\b/d'
